we hauen iended the furste partie
in the whiche it is itreted
schewynge wherinne kynges and princes schullen sette here felicite.
ende in riches nother in cyuil myght and strengthe,
but he schal vse al suche thinges,
as it is proued totore atte folle,
as that dede cometh ot charite.
For thanne kynges hauen felicite accordyng to here astate,
whanne by excityng of [d]eleccioun of God,
that bien vnder hem holilyche and rightefulliche
thanne the felicite of kynges schal
And by knowyng and loue of hym he schal ordeyne
in what verues kynges schal be excellent and vertuous
therfore it nedeth firste to schewe
poten|cie and myghtes ot the soule
and in whiche myghtes the vertues
And thanne it schal be ischewed
how many suche vertues ther bien
and thanne we schal trete singulerlich of eueriche ot these vertues
and how it semeth kynges and princes
ot the soule mowe be destyngued
For som bien kynde and natural,
as in vertual myght ot norsching, of wexinge,
as corden to herbes and treen.
and felyng be the sight, tast, and touche
and therinne we accordeth wip other bestes.
for oon appetit is in man
as the appetit that foloweth the intellectual inwit.
And another maner appetit is in man and in beste,
as the appetite that folweth the felyng,
and suche appetite may be cleped sensualitas.
And the appetit that folweth the intellectual inwit
And in that manere of speche other bestes
that hatte sensualitas and appetitus sensitiuus,
the intellectual wille thanne the vertues
bien in the natural vertual myghtes,
other in the vertual myghtes and wittes ot felyng,
other in the appetite sensitif ot felyng,
other in the intellecte and inwyt,
and that is declared in thre wise.
nother iblamed for naturel and kynde vertue,
for no man is acounted a good man
for he hath good degestioun and wexinge.
the seconde, suche vertues beth nought
for suche vertues stonden by resoun
For thei a man make resouns
properliche for to speke the natural potencial vertues varieth not in here kynde worchynge
but wercheth alwey with al here myght.
bien not in natural uertual myghtes,
suche habites and vertues inclineth potencial vertues
and as a vertuous habite inclineth to do wel.
and potencial vertues bien fol inclyned
by kynde to worche by here owne kynde,
and so therby we beth nother vertuous ne vicious
and so therinne may not be suche vertues.
And by the same thre resouns
by the whiche it is preued
bien not in the natural potencial vertues,
it may be preued that thei bien not in the knowyng
For as we ben nought ipreysed nother iblamed
so we ben not ipreysed nother iblamed
for as no man is preysed
for he hath a gode digestion and wexyng,
yf a man is ble|r eyghed and his sight apeired
other ghif he hath euel digestioun or feblenesse of stomachus [for etyng,
aliter] he schold be blamed [for ouer mych drinkyng,
aliter] and nought for he hath yuel dygestioun,
in oure power propurliche for to speke
to muche ot mete and of drynke,
for it was in his powere to ete and drynke mesurableliche.
is not in the potencial vertue
for as the kynde potencial vertues
no man hath more digestioun other lasse,
for it is not in manis power
to se other heitlerlich other dymlich;
thanne vertue is not in fife wittes,
moral vertue is not in the fif wittes;
for as the natural potencial vertues beth fol set
and is therfore nother preysed nother iblamed,
therinne may no moral vertue be iset.
potencial vertue is iset awork atte folle
and nedeth therfore no moral vertue.
And so moral vertue is not in natural potencial vertue
ben there nought bute the intellectualle -vertu,
and the appetite sensytyue and intellectiue.
